title: The Hermit and the Outlaw and Three Messengers of Death (Kaluza selection)
summary: CME00080 is the repository copy of a University of Michigan encoding of two non-contiguous poem texts from Max Kaluza's 1890 article 'Kleinere Publikationen aus me. Handschriften.' The Hermit and the Outlaw (Kaluza pp. 171–177) was transcribed from British Library Add MS 22577, William Fillingham's c. 1806 paper transcript of the surviving fifteenth-century source now Add MS 37492, ff. 76v–82v. Thomas Park had already printed the complete poem, including its 24-line prologue, from a separate Fillingham transcript in 1816. Three Messengers of Death (Kaluza pp. 184–187) was transcribed from the late-fourteenth-century Simeon Manuscript, BL Add MS 22283, ff. 88vb–89ra, with limited comparison against the parallel Vernon Manuscript, Bodleian MS Eng. poet. a.1, ff. 297vc–298rb. Horstmann had printed the Vernon form in 1887. Complete scans of the three historical print volumes, the complete DIMEV 1.0 release, a complete modern METS edition, and Digital Bodleian's complete 710-canvas Vernon project are cached locally. The full Vernon bundle retains covers, endleaves, and every exposed manuscript surface; one-based canvases 481–482 map the work. None of the three relevant British Library objects currently has an obtainable complete public facsimile: Add MS 22283's legacy images are explicitly unavailable, and Add MSS 22577 and 37492 expose catalog records but no Digitised Content. Whole-object Imaging Services request routes and image-reuse restrictions are recorded separately.

entities.label: General Corpus source XML for CME00080
entities.description: The XML artifact consumed by this repository's publication workflow and supplied through the Kyvero-Press/CME git submodule.
notes: IDG/@ID, BIBNO, and VID all identify CME00080; the file is 36,568 bytes.
The XML has two DIV1 poem containers but no bibliographic header naming Kaluza or the 1890 article.

entities.label: University of Michigan Corpus of Middle English item CME00080
entities.description: A public keyed transcription of the two poem texts selected from Kaluza 1890. It retains printed page breaks, Fillingham-transcript folio milestones in the first poem, editorial notes, line groups, and two Latin quotation blocks in the second poem, while omitting most of the article's German editorial prose.

entities.label: Max Kaluza, 'Kleinere Publikationen aus me. Handschriften' (1890)
entities.description: The immediate print source. CME00080 encodes the first poem's text on pp. 171–177 and the second poem's text on pp. 184–187, not Kaluza's complete pp. 165–187 article.

bibliographic.citation: Max Kaluza, 'Kleinere Publikationen aus me. Handschriften,' Englische Studien 14 (1890): 165–187.
bibliographic.title: Kleinere Publikationen aus me. Handschriften
bibliographic.container_title: Englische Studien: Organ für englische Philologie

notes: Section I is headed 'The Eremyte and the Outelawe' and identifies Add MS 22577; Section II is headed 'Die Boten des Todes' and identifies Add MS 22283.
Kaluza's statements that both poems were previously unpublished were already false in 1890: Park printed the first in 1816 and Horstmann the second in 1887.

entities.label: Internet Archive complete scan of Englische Studien volume 14 (1890)
entities.description: A complete 496-page PDF of the journal volume. Kaluza's full article is at PDF pages 175–197; the CME00080 poem texts occupy PDF pages 181–187 and 194–197.

entities.label: British Library Add MS 22577
alternate_labels: William Fillingham transcript of The Eremyte and the Owtelawe
entities.description: The c. 1806 paper working transcript used directly by Kaluza for The Hermit and the Outlaw. William Fillingham copied it from the medieval manuscript now Add MS 37492 and presented it to Philip Bliss.

physical_description.extent: 1 item; exact leaf count not stated in the current public record
notes: The catalog separates 363 narrative lines from a preceding, miscounted 23-line introduction; Kaluza establishes that the introduction has 24 lines and is integral.
Kaluza's transcription includes folio milestones from 3a through 15a, but their exact concordance with current BL foliation has not been visually verified.

entities.label: British Library Add MS 37492
entities.description: The surviving medieval source of The Hermit and the Outlaw, copied by J. Gage and formerly owned by William Fillingham. The poem occupies ff. 76v–82v within a larger paper volume of romantic and religious poems.

physical_description.format: Codex of romantic and religious poems
notes: Written by J. Gage.
The present object was in William Fillingham's possession in 1805; the current catalog identifies it confidently with the source described as vellum in Bliss's note to Add MS 22577.

entities.label: Fillingham transcript used for Park's 1816 edition
entities.description: A transcript that Thomas Park says William Fillingham extracted from his manuscript volume and allowed Park to use. The current Add MS 37492 catalog calls it a transcript of that manuscript and distinguishes it from Add MS 22577, another Fillingham transcript.

holding.notes: No current shelfmark or survival evidence was found. This entity uses the manuscript_witness schema type provisionally for a material working transcript.

notes: Do not identify this physical transcript with Add MS 22577 without further provenance evidence.

entities.label: Thomas Park, 'The Eremyte and the Owtelawe' (1816)
entities.description: The first known complete publication of the poem, including its 24-line opening before 'Off tweye brethyrn.' Park printed from a Fillingham transcript and retained acknowledged imperfections rather than conjecturally supplying them.

bibliographic.citation: Thomas Park, ed., 'The Eremyte and the Owtelawe. A Balade,' in Sir Egerton Brydges, Restituta; or, Titles, Extracts, and Characters of Old Books in English Literature, Revived, vol. 4 (London: T. Bensley for Longman, Hurst, Rees, Orme, and Brown, 1816), 91–104.
bibliographic.title: The Eremyte and the Owtelawe. A Balade
bibliographic.container_title: Restituta; or, Titles, Extracts, and Characters of Old Books in English Literature, Revived
bibliographic.publisher: T. Bensley for Longman, Hurst, Rees, Orme, and Brown

entities.label: Internet Archive complete scan of Restituta volume 4 (1816)
entities.description: A complete 604-page PDF of volume 4; Park's edition occupies PDF pages 113–126.

entities.label: British Library Add MS 22283 (Simeon Manuscript)
entities.description: The late-fourteenth-century parchment codex directly transcribed by Kaluza for Three Messengers of Death. The poem occupies ff. 88vb–89ra and shares its terminal column with the following Song of Love-Longing to Jesus.

physical_description.extent: 172 folios, plus five unfoliated modern paper leaves at the beginning, 24 at the end, and five inserted between specified folio pairs
notes: The manuscript is closely associated in production and contents with the Vernon Manuscript.
Its current record requires a letter of introduction for onsite consultation.

entities.label: Bodleian Library MS Eng. poet. a.1 (Vernon Manuscript)
entities.description: The parallel late-fourteenth-century Vernon witness of Three Messengers of Death at ff. 297vc–298rb. Kaluza examined it cursorily for variants; Horstmann and the modern METS edition use it as their base.

physical_description.extent: viii + 342 + i surviving leaves; incomplete, originally viii + 413 with 71 leaves lost
physical_description.format: Large, richly decorated double- and triple-column devotional codex
notes: Digital Bodleian gives England, West Midlands? as the place of origin and describes the manuscript as an extensive collection of Middle English religious and didactic texts.
The work begins partway down the third column of fol. 297v and ends in the second column of fol. 298r, with adjacent works on both surfaces.

entities.label: Digital Bodleian complete Vernon Manuscript project
entities.description: The provider's complete 710-canvas published project inventory, from the upper cover through the spine and lower cover. Every canvas was cached as a practical 1600-pixel color access derivative; the work is on one-based inventory positions 481–482.
entities.reproduction_format: IIIF Presentation 2 manifest / 710 color JPEG access derivatives

entities.label: Carl Horstmann, Vernon edition of Three Messengers of Death (1887)
entities.description: The first known publication of the poem, printed from the Vernon Manuscript as section 5 of Horstmann's 'Nachträge zu den Legenden.'

bibliographic.citation: Carl Horstmann, 'Nachträge zu den Legenden,' section 5, '(The messengers of Death),' Archiv für das Studium der neueren Sprachen und Litteraturen 79 (1887): 432–434.
bibliographic.title: Nachträge zu den Legenden. 5. (The messengers of Death)
bibliographic.container_title: Archiv für das Studium der neueren Sprachen und Litteraturen

entities.label: Internet Archive complete scan of Archiv volume 79 (1887)
entities.description: A complete 500-page PDF of the volume; Horstmann's Three Messengers text occupies PDF pages 446–448.

entities.label: Cook and Strakhov, Three Messengers of Death (METS, 2019)
entities.description: A modern open-access reading edition based on Vernon and collated with Simeon, within John Lydgate's Dance of Death and Related Works. It prints the two Latin distichs as four short lines each and therefore numbers 224 lines / 56 quatrains.

bibliographic.citation: Megan L. Cook and Elizaveta Strakhov, eds., John Lydgate's Dance of Death and Related Works (Kalamazoo: Medieval Institute Publications, 2019), 65–71, 136–137, 192–193.
bibliographic.title: John Lydgate's Dance of Death and Related Works

entities.label: Richard Firth Green, The Hermit and the Outlaw: An Edition (2003)
entities.description: A modern scholarly edition in Interstices. The publisher page supplies bibliographic access but not anonymous full text; it is a later supporting edition, not a source of CME00080.

date_statements.label: Publication
date_statements.notes: Some print bibliographies cite 2004; the current publisher DOI page gives 2003.

bibliographic.citation: Richard Firth Green, 'The Hermit and the Outlaw: An Edition,' in Interstices: Studies in Late Middle English and Anglo-Latin Texts in Honour of A. G. Rigg, ed. Richard Firth Green and Linne R. Mooney (Toronto: University of Toronto Press, 2003), 137–166, https://doi.org/10.3138/9781442676268-012.
bibliographic.title: The Hermit and the Outlaw: An Edition
bibliographic.container_title: Interstices: Studies in Late Middle English and Anglo-Latin Texts in Honour of A. G. Rigg

entities.label: DIMEV 1.0 records 440 and 5387
entities.description: Versioned scholarly index records for The Hermit and the Outlaw and Three Messengers of Death, documenting incipits, witnesses, loci, and editions.

locators: The Eremyte and the Outelawe, printed pp. 171–177
Die Boten des Todes, printed pp. 184–187

scope.notes: CME omits the article's pp. 165–170 and 178–184 editorial discussion while retaining textual notes attached to the poem transcriptions.

subject.description: The Hermit and the Outlaw edition
Complete c. 1806 transcript of the poem

object.description: Fillingham transcript made available to Park

subject.description: Limited Vernon variants in Die Boten des Todes

scope.notes: Kaluza says he only examined Vernon cursorily because its variants were almost entirely graphic; Simeon remains the base.

object.description: Complete provider-published manuscript project, including exterior surfaces

editorial_practices.representation: Two poem texts at pp. 171–177 and 184–187
editorial_practices.description: The encoding excludes most German introduction, argument, dialect discussion, and explanatory notes between and around the poems while retaining the transcribed verse and many textual notes.

editorial_practices.representation: Corrected reading with Fillingham's rejected forms in notes
editorial_practices.description: Kaluza reports moving obvious Fillingham misreadings to notes, omitting the transcript's marginal modern-English glosses, joining the 24-line introduction to the poem, and restoring vv. 61–78 before vv. 79–96 because a transcript leaf had been misbound.

editorial_practices.source_feature: Simeon spelling and manuscript stanza initials
editorial_practices.representation: Simeon-based text with initial-letter regulation and a small number of Vernon variants
editorial_practices.description: Kaluza says he follows Simeon's orthography except for regulating initial letters and only cursorily compares Vernon because most variants are graphical.

editorial_practices.source_feature: Two Latin distichs visually wrapped across four short lines each in Kaluza's narrow columns
editorial_practices.representation: Two Q blocks containing two logical L elements each
editorial_practices.description: CME counts four logical Latin hexameter lines and 216 Middle English lines (220 L total). Cook and Strakhov divide the Latin at the caesura, count eight Latin half-lines, and number 224 lines / 56 quatrains. No textual omission was found.

No public complete page-image object was found. The exact catalog exposes no Digitised Content, and Add MS 37492 is absent from the 10 October 2025 complete digitised-manuscript list.

The exact catalog links a legacy Digitised Manuscripts record but explicitly states that its digital images are currently unavailable; Add MS 22283 is absent from the 10 October 2025 complete digitised-manuscript list.

Use the British Library Imaging Services form. Request the complete current paper object—covers, spine, boards or pastedowns if applicable, flyleaves, blanks, and every recto and verso—and identify The Hermit and the Outlaw separately as the full transcript, associated by Kaluza with folio milestones 3a–15a. Ask the Library to confirm current foliation and complete-object extent.

rights.component: U-M electronic text and public item representation
rights.basis: The current U-M Corpus of Middle English collection page states that its materials are public domain in the United States and applies the DPLA No Copyright–United States statement.
notes: The statement is jurisdiction-specific and does not determine rights in independently supplied manuscript photographs or every interface asset.

rights.basis: Anonymous medieval works first published no later than 1816 and 1887.
notes: This does not determine rights in modern editorial text, XML markup, photographs, or provider-delivered files.

rights.component: Internet Archive complete-volume PDF and scan derivatives
rights.basis: The historical printed volume is public domain in the United States, but no item-specific open license for the scan file was established in this review.
notes: Public download and local research caching are recorded separately from permission to redistribute the provider's exact scan.

rights.component: Internet Archive complete-volume PDF and scan derivatives
rights.basis: The historical printed volume is public domain in the United States, but no item-specific open license for the scan file was established in this review.
notes: Public download and local research caching are recorded separately from permission to redistribute the provider's exact scan.

rights.component: Google-digitized complete-volume PDF distributed by Internet Archive
rights.basis: The historical printed volume is public domain in the United States, while the file's embedded Google notice asks that the digitized file be used for personal, non-commercial purposes.
notes: The restriction concerns the provider scan file, not the underlying 1887 edition text.

rights.basis: The exact IIIF manifest attributes the photographs to Bodleian Libraries and applies CC BY-NC 4.0.
notes: Non-commercial redistribution with attribution is permitted by the item license; commercial print-on-demand or other commercial reuse requires separate permission.

rights.component: DIMEV version 1.0 data and repository contents
rights.basis: The release README and bundled LICENSE apply Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International.

rights.component: METS digital edition and downloadable PDF text
rights.basis: METS states that digital editions are for individual use only and directs reprint requests to its Executive Director.
notes: METS separately licenses its XML under CC BY-NC-SA 4.0. That XML license should not be generalized to the rendered web text or PDF, and no exact XML deliverable was cached here.

rights.basis: Modern University of Toronto Press chapter with access restricted by the publisher platform.

rights.component: Any photographs supplied through British Library Imaging Services

evidence.citation: CME00080 XML source in the pinned Kyvero-Press/CME submodule.
evidence.locator: IDG, BIBNO, VID, BODY/DIV1 1–2, HEAD, PB, MILESTONE, LG, Q, L, and NOTE
evidence.summary: Confirms artifact identity, the two selected poem divisions, 607 L and 88 LG metrics, printed-page and transcript-folio milestones, and the exact incipits and explicits. File SHA-256, git blob, and pinned CME commit were verified.

evidence.citation: University of Michigan Library Digital Collections, CME00080.
evidence.summary: Confirms the public U-M item identifier and digital-text access route associated with the repository XML. The current collection page states that CME materials are public domain in the United States and applies the DPLA No Copyright–United States statement.

evidence.citation: Max Kaluza, 'Kleinere Publikationen aus me. Handschriften,' Englische Studien 14 (1890): 165–187, audited in the complete volume scan.
evidence.locator: PDF pp. 175–197; especially printed pp. 165–171, 177–184, and 187
evidence.summary: Confirms the title, author, imprint, complete article boundary, exact encoded page runs, Kaluza's direct source statements, Fillingham-transcript corrections and reordering, incomplete stanzas and lacuna, tentative dates and dialects, Simeon base and limited Vernon comparison, and 54-quatrain / 216-numbered-line treatment of the English stanzas in Three Messengers. qpdf passes all 496 pages.

evidence.citation: Thomas Park, ed., 'The Eremyte and the Owtelawe. A Balade,' Restituta 4 (1816): 91–104, audited in the complete volume scan.
evidence.summary: Confirms the 1816 title and publication, the poem's complete opening with the 24-line prologue and its final explicit, Park's acknowledgement that Fillingham supplied a transcript extracted from his manuscript volume, and Park's decision not to conjecturally supply imperfections. qpdf passes all 604 pages.

evidence.citation: Carl Horstmann, 'Nachträge zu den Legenden,' section 5, '(The messengers of Death),' Archiv 79 (1887): 432–434, audited in the complete volume scan.
evidence.summary: Confirms the 1887 imprint and Horstmann's complete Vernon-based Three Messengers text on pp. 432–434, including the manuscript heading, Latin verses, lineation, and ending. qpdf passes all 500 pages.

evidence.citation: British Library Archives and Manuscripts Catalogue, Add MS 22577, record 040-002096544.
evidence.locator: Scope and Content, date, format, extent, access, and blank Digitised Content field
evidence.summary: Confirms the c. 1806 paper small-quarto transcript, Fillingham's gift to Philip Bliss, the quoted source note, catalog line-count split, one-item extent, current shelfmark, and absence of an exposed digital object.

evidence.citation: British Library Archives and Manuscripts Catalogue, Add MS 37492, record 032-002053858.
evidence.locator: Item 3, ff. 76v–82v; physical description; custody; edition history; blank Digitised Content field
evidence.summary: Confirms the exact poem locus, incipit, explicit, second-half-fifteenth-century date, J. Gage hand, paper support, iv + 92 folio extent, dimensions, Fillingham provenance, physical identity with the source of Add MS 22577, and distinct transcript routes for Park and Kaluza.

evidence.citation: British Library Archives and Manuscripts Catalogue, Add MS 22283 (Simeon Manuscript), record 032-002034756.
evidence.locator: ff. 88vb–89ra, date, physical description, access conditions, and Digitised Content statement
evidence.summary: Confirms the Three Messengers title, locus, incipit, explicit, late-fourteenth-century date, parchment support, dimensions, foliation and inserted leaves, letter-of-introduction requirement, and that the linked legacy digital images are currently unavailable.

evidence.citation: Digital Bodleian IIIF manifest and complete local bundle for the Vernon Manuscript, audited 2026-07-12.
evidence.locator: All 710 canvases inventoried; ff. 297v–298r at one-based positions 481–482
evidence.summary: The manifest confirms the 1381–1400 date, West Midlands? origin, viii + 342 + i surviving extent, 544 × 393 mm dimensions, and 710 canvases. ZIP integrity passes; all 710 image members and 960,063,674 image bytes match the inventory hashes. Visual inspection confirms the work beginning partway down fol. 297vc and ending on fol. 298rb, with both surfaces shared by adjacent texts.
notes: The separately cached source manifest has SHA-256 90d113e82acf31163ce926ee38f342b6fbd3162b7d976ec744e73905c42e57bc.

evidence.citation: Digital Bodleian Vernon Manuscript IIIF attribution and license statement.
evidence.summary: Attributes photographs to Bodleian Libraries and applies CC BY-NC 4.0 with a link to Digital Bodleian terms.

evidence.citation: Digital Index of Middle English Verse, version 1.0 source-data release, records 440 and 5387.
evidence.summary: Versioned scholarly index data confirms both work identities, incipits, surviving witness and transcript loci, and edition references. The bundled README and LICENSE apply CC BY 4.0. ZIP integrity passes at release commit 5ad5f592868471b44fc72f6e2f42db419780b42c.

evidence.citation: Megan L. Cook and Elizaveta Strakhov, eds., John Lydgate's Dance of Death and Related Works (METS, 2019), introduction, text, and notes for Three Messengers of Death.
evidence.summary: Confirms anonymous authorship, the title derived from Vernon's rubric, 56-quatrain modern lineation, West Midlands dialect, Vernon base at ff. 297vc–298rb, Simeon witness at ff. 88vb–89ra, Horstmann 1887 first edition, macaronic Latin passages, death-poetry context, and variant collation. qpdf passes all 215 pages.

evidence.citation: Middle English Texts Series, Copyright & Citation.
evidence.summary: States that digital editions are provided for individual use only and that reprinting digital text requires contact with the Executive Director; separately licenses XML under CC BY-NC-SA 4.0.

evidence.citation: Richard Firth Green, 'The Hermit and the Outlaw: An Edition,' Interstices (University of Toronto Press, 2003), 137–166.
evidence.summary: The current publisher record confirms the chapter title, author, editors, container, publisher, date, page range, DOI, and restricted full-text access.

evidence.citation: British Library, Digitised manuscripts and archives shelfmark list, updated 10 October 2025.
evidence.summary: The complete 261-page provider list of then-available digitised manuscripts does not include any of the three British Library objects, corroborating the exact catalog checks. The PDF opens and text-extracts; non-fatal linearization hint warnings do not prevent page access.

agents.name: Digital Index of Middle English Verse

open_questions.question: Does the separate Fillingham transcript used by Thomas Park survive, and can it be identified with any current shelfmark?
next_steps: Trace Park's papers and Fillingham provenance records.
Compare Park's readings with Add MS 22577 and Add MS 37492 before proposing physical identity.

open_questions.question: What is the exact current foliation and whole-object surface inventory of Add MS 22577?
next_steps: Ask British Library Imaging Services to confirm the complete leaf count and current recto/verso labels before ordering.
Crosswalk Kaluza's 3a–15a milestones to the delivered images without assuming a modern foliation.

open_questions.question: Will the British Library restore public access to the legacy complete images for Add MS 22283?
next_steps: Monitor the exact catalog's Digitised Content field and the provider's digitised-manuscript list.
Meanwhile request a whole-object color reproduction and written reuse terms through Imaging Services.

open_questions.question: Can complete color research facsimiles and publication permission be obtained for Add MSS 22577, 37492, and 22283 at a practical cost?
next_steps: Submit separate whole-object quote requests using the exact surface scopes in each access record.
Ask permissions@bl.uk for explicit written terms covering repository publication, scholarship, and any commercial print-on-demand reuse before redistributing supplied images.
notes: Published per-image rates are not a reliable total for these large, fragile objects; the current terms make large orders subject to individual discussion.

open_questions.question: What specific source tradition or analogue underlies The Hermit and the Outlaw?
next_steps: Consult Green's complete 2003 edition and current exemplum scholarship.
Do not turn Kaluza's general Latin-or-French-source conjecture into an upstream lineage entity without a specific textual identification.

review_notes: The two poems are separate intellectual works and every witness or edition relation is part-scoped.
Park 1816 and Horstmann 1887 are prior editions, not sources of Kaluza's readings.
The complete Vernon facsimile is cached and mapped to the exact columns; the British Library negative findings are explicitly dated and requestable rather than treated as evidence of loss.

No new reusable modeling problem was found beyond the skill's existing rules for edited selections, intermediary transcripts, lineation differences, complete-codex caching, and provider-specific image rights.
